title: Counterfeit Coins I

source.file: content/A Practical Guide To Quantitative Finance Interviews Book by Xinfeng Zhou (chapters)/chapter-02-brain-teasers.md

setup.narrative: There are 10 bags, each containing 100 coins. Nine of the bags contain GENUINE coins weighing exactly 10 grams each. ONE bag contains COUNTERFEIT coins weighing either 9 grams OR 11 grams each (you don't know which way).
rules: You have a digital scale that gives a precise weight reading (not just balance comparison).
You may use the scale only ONCE.
You can place any number of coins from any bags on the scale.
You must identify which bag is counterfeit AND determine whether it's heavier or lighter.
setup.question: What single weighing identifies the counterfeit bag?

learningObjectives: Use a series-summation insight to encode bag identity into a single readable number.
Recognize that proportional sampling (j coins from bag j) makes the deviation distinguishable for each bag.
Read both the bag identity AND the direction of the offset from one weighing.

stages.label: Why naive approaches fail
stages.prompt: Suppose you weigh ONE coin from each bag (10 coins, 1 per bag). What does the total weight tell you?

options.text: The deviation from 100 is +/- 1 -- you know there's a counterfeit but you can't tell WHICH bag.
It identifies the counterfeit bag uniquely.
Nothing -- you'd need to weigh each coin separately.

stages.explanation: Equal sampling means every bag contributes the same way. The deviation tells you THERE IS a counterfeit (and which direction) but not which bag. You need UNEQUAL sampling that encodes bag identity.

stages.label: Choose your technique

options.text: Series summation: take a different NUMBER of coins from each bag, so the deviation reveals the bag's index.
Probability over which bag is counterfeit.

stages.explanation: Encode each bag's identity into the count of coins taken: 1 from bag 1, 2 from bag 2, ..., 10 from bag 10. The total of 1+2+...+10 = 55 coins. The deviation from the expected weight directly identifies the counterfeit bag.

stages.label: Expected weight
stages.prompt: If all 55 coins (1 from bag 1, 2 from bag 2, ..., 10 from bag 10) were genuine 10-gram coins, what would the scale read?

stages.label: Read the deviation
stages.prompt: Suppose the scale reads 553 grams. What do you conclude?

options.text: Bag 3 is counterfeit, and its coins are HEAVIER (11 g each). Each of 3 counterfeits adds 1 gram, so total +3.
Bag 3 is counterfeit, and its coins are LIGHTER (9 g each).
It's ambiguous between bag 3-heavier and bag 7-lighter.

stages.explanation: Deviation = 553 - 550 = +3 grams. Sign positive => counterfeit is HEAVIER. Magnitude = 3 => 3 counterfeits contributed, meaning bag j with j = 3. So bag 3 is counterfeit and heavy.

stages.label: Commit
stages.prompt: Does this strategy work for both directions (counterfeit heavier OR lighter) in a single weighing?

options.text: Yes -- the SIGN of the deviation gives the direction; the MAGNITUDE gives the bag index. Range: -10 to +10 (excluding 0).
Only if you know the direction in advance.
Only for the heavier case -- lighter would make some weights negative.

stages.explanation: 20 distinct non-zero deviations are possible: -1 to -10 (lighter, bags 1-10) and +1 to +10 (heavier, bags 1-10). Each maps to a unique (bag, direction) pair.

stages.label: Reflection
prompts: What if there were 100 bags? How many coins total would you weigh?
What if TWO bags contained counterfeits with different deviations? Could you still identify both in one weighing?
Generalize: how does this idea relate to error-correcting codes?
